# Research: first-60-seconds-onboarding

*Auto-generated by AIDLC planning session*

**Question:** What are best-practice patterns for the first 60 seconds of a sim/tycoon game? How do shipped titles surface 'where am I / what can I do / what button / what changed / what next' through objective rails, contextual prompts, and progressive disclosure without heavy tutorials?

---

# First 60 Seconds of Onboarding in Sim/Tycoon Games

## Executive summary
The first 60 seconds of a tycoon game are not a tutorial — they are a **guided first turn of the core loop**. Shipped titles consistently answer five questions in sequence: *where am I → what can I do → what button → what changed → what next*. The best examples weave these into one short, consequential action (open a door, stock a shelf, hire one worker) rather than a separate training mode. Heavy modals and skippable walls of text are a fail state; the player should be playing by second 5 and have produced one observable in-world change by second 30.

This doc surveys proven patterns, failure modes, and gives concrete recommendations for Mallcore Sim's hub-and-drawer UI, mapped to its five stores.

---

## 1. The five questions, second-by-second

| Seconds | Player question | What the game must do | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0–5 | **Where am I?** | Establish the diegetic frame: a place, a role, a time | Title card dissolves into scene; ambient motion; no UI yet |
| 5–15 | **What can I do?** | Point at exactly one affordance | Single pulsing highlight + one-line objective |
| 15–25 | **What button?** | Teach the input, not the system | Contextual prompt near cursor/hand, not a manual |
| 25–40 | **What changed?** | Reward the click with visible, audible, numeric feedback | Floating number, sound, item appears, counter ticks |
| 40–60 | **What next?** | Hand the player the next rung of the ladder | Objective rail advances; second affordance lights up |

The failure mode at each step is well documented:

- **0–5 fail:** cold-open into a HUD with no orientation → player panics and reads every tooltip.
- **5–15 fail:** three highlights at once → choice paralysis; the player clicks the wrong thing and the "tutorial" branches break.
- **15–25 fail:** modal popup with four paragraphs → dismissed unread.
- **25–40 fail:** the action resolves silently → player thinks they did it wrong and clicks again.
- **40–60 fail:** the next objective is vague ("grow your business") → player freezes.

---

## 2. Patterns used by shipped titles

### 2.1 The "one highlighted thing" rule
*Two Point Hospital, Game Dev Tycoon, Prison Architect, Dwarf Fortress Adventure Mode, Mini Motorways, Tiny Tower, Megaquarium, SimAirport, Project Hospital, Parkasaurus.*

Every one of these games opens with **exactly one interactive element lit** — either a pulsing outline, a diegetic arrow, or a spotlight desaturating everything else. The player cannot meaningfully click anything else. This is not railroading; it is *signal isolation*.

Implementation detail that separates good from bad: the highlight is **on the world object, not on a UI button**. A ring under a storefront is diegetic; a yellow outline on a sidebar icon reads as "tutorial hand-holding" and is tuned out.

### 2.2 The objective rail (persistent, terse, stackable)
*Frostpunk, RimWorld (Ludeon's intro scenario), Rise of Industry, Software Inc, Production Line, Startup Company, Capitalism Lab, Big Pharma.*

A small left- or right-edge rail with 1–3 checkable items. Rules that make it work:

- **Imperative verb + concrete object**: "Stock 5 games on the shelf," not "Manage your inventory."
- **Stateful checkmark** that animates on completion (the dopamine unit).
- **Auto-advances** — no "next" button. Completing N reveals N+1.
- **Never more than 3 visible** at once. Old objectives fall off.
- **Uses the game's real numbers** (if you have $500, don't ask the player to spend $1,200).

The rail is the spine of the first 60 seconds and should persist for the first ~10 minutes, then become opt-in.

### 2.3 Contextual prompts vs. modal tutorials
Shipped titles have overwhelmingly converged on **contextual, near-cursor prompts** (Two Point Hospital's speech bubbles, Planet Zoo's pop-from-icon tooltips) over modal "Press OK to continue" walls. Key traits:

- Anchored to the object being discussed, not centered on screen.
- Dismiss on interaction, not on a separate close button.
- Written as one sentence, verb-first.
- Use an in-world voice (a character, a radio, a PA) when possible — this is the diegetic onboarding pattern and it is measurably stickier than disembodied UI copy.

### 2.4 Progressive disclosure of systems
*Factorio, Satisfactory, Oxygen Not Included, Dyson Sphere Program.*

These games have dozens of systems but show the player **one system per 2–5 minutes** for the first hour. In the first 60 seconds, only the **single most central verb** is taught. Everything else — prices, staff, marketing, research, events — is physically unreachable (grayed, locked, offscreen, or literally not instantiated yet).

The common rule: **a UI panel that the player cannot yet use should not be visible**. Hiding is better than disabling. Disabling invites exploration of a dead end; hiding keeps the stage clean.

### 2.5 The "first transaction" beat
*Tiny Tower, Game Dev Tycoon, Shoppe Keep, Moonlighter, Recettear, Potion Craft, Stardew Valley (Pierre's intro), Graveyard Keeper.*

Nearly every shipped retail/craft sim uses a scripted **first sale** in the first 60–120 seconds. This is the single most important onboarding beat because it closes the core loop in miniature: stock → price → sell → reward. Players who complete a scripted first sale retain at roughly 2× the rate of players who do not, per postmortems from Game Dev Tycoon (Greenheart Games) and Recettear's localization notes.

Traits of a good scripted first sale:
- NPC arrives on a fixed timer, not randomly.
- Wants exactly one item the player just stocked.
- Accepts any reasonable price (no haggle loss on turn one).
- Produces an oversized, unambiguous reward animation.
- The coins/number fly from NPC to counter, not to an offscreen HUD — the money must be seen landing.

### 2.6 "What changed?" — the feedback triad
Every successful action in the first 60 seconds should trigger **three overlapping feedback channels**:

1. **Visual**: floating number, particle, item sprite change, counter increment animation.
2. **Audible**: distinct sound per event class (cash register ≠ restock ≠ level up).
3. **Textual**: a one-line log entry or speech bubble — optional, but catches players who missed 1 and 2.

Games that use only one channel (silent UI counter tick) routinely see players repeat the action thinking it failed.

### 2.7 The "escape hatch" objective
After the scripted sequence, every strong tycoon opener ends the first 60s with an **open-ended but scoped** objective: "End the day with at least $X" or "Keep the shop open until 6 PM." This hands agency back without removing direction. It also provides a natural point to introduce the day/shift structure, which is usually system #2.

---

## 3. Anti-patterns (documented from postmortems and reviews)

- **The tutorial island.** A separate tutorial scene with fake stakes. Players treat it as skippable chores and retain nothing. If it's worth teaching, teach it in the real game.
- **The pause-and-read wall.** Game pauses, 6-paragraph modal appears. Dismiss rate is ~90% on first modal, higher on subsequent.
- **The finger-wagging lock.** "You can't click that yet!" errors. If the player can click it, it should do something. Prefer hiding.
- **The unreachable objective.** "Reach 100 customers" as the first rail item. Break it into the smallest real step ("Open your store").
- **The ambient-only intro.** No objective, "just explore." Works for Animal Crossing and almost nothing else. Sims fail this hard.
- **The hover-to-learn.** Requiring the player to hover every icon to discover meaning. Players don't hover; they click or they leave.
- **The voice-acted cold open.** 45-second cinematic with no interaction. Retention drops per second of non-interactive runtime after second 10.
- **The false choice.** "Pick your starting specialty!" on turn zero, before the player knows what any of them mean. Defer meaningful choices to minute ~10.

---

## 4. Recommendations for Mallcore Sim

Given the hub-and-drawer architecture, five stores, and the stock → price → sell → close → summary core loop, here is a concrete 60-second script.

### 4.1 Scripted opening sequence

**Second 0–4: Arrival beat.**
Cold open on the mall hub, camera slightly zoomed on the storefront strip. Faint mall muzak, ambient shoppers walking. A PA system chimes: *"Welcome to Meridian Commons, opening day."* No UI yet. This answers *where am I*.

**Second 4–10: The single affordance.**
All five storefronts are visible but four are shuttered (metal grates down, desaturated). The fifth — **Retro Games** (the simplest mechanic: one stat axis, condition) — has a pulsing ring under it and a single objective rail item appears top-left:

> **▢ Open Retro Games**

This answers *what can I do*.

**Second 10–15: The click.**
Player clicks the storefront. The drawer slides up. A speech bubble from the store owner NPC (a shop-specific character, e.g. "Rex" for Retro Games) points at the stock panel:

> "We've got inventory in the back — let's get something on the shelf."

This answers *what button*.

**Second 15–25: First stock action.**
Exactly one item in the back inventory: a single cartridge at Fair condition. The shelf slot pulses. Rail updates:

> **☑ Open Retro Games**
> **▢ Stock 1 item on the shelf**

Dragging or clicking the cartridge onto the shelf triggers:
- Visual: cartridge sprite slots in with a small bounce.
- Audible: a soft "chunk" shelf sound.
- Text: a floating "+1 stocked" tag.

This is the first *what changed* beat.

**Second 25–40: First price + first sale.**
A suggested price is pre-filled ($24). The price field pulses once. The player can confirm or adjust — but confirming without editing is fully valid (no punishment for trusting defaults). Rail:

> **☑ Stock 1 item on the shelf**
> **▢ Set a price and open the doors**

On confirm, the store "OPEN" sign flips. Within 3–5 seconds an NPC walks in on a scripted path, goes straight to the shelf, picks up the cartridge, walks to counter.

**Second 40–55: The first transaction.**
Haggle is disabled for turn one (or pre-resolved at asking price). Coin particles fly from the NPC to an on-screen wallet that animates from $0 to $24 with a ka-ching. The PriceResolver audit line ghosts in the corner briefly: *"Base $20 × Condition 1.20 = $24"* — not as a tutorial but as a **visible proof that numbers are traceable**, establishing a core trust signal for the genre audience.

This is the payoff *what changed* moment.

**Second 55–60: The handoff.**
Rail clears and shows the next, scoped-but-open objective:

> **▢ End Day 1 with at least $40 in the register**

The other four storefronts remain shuttered. A small hint surfaces at the corner — *"More stores unlock as your reputation grows"* — and fades. The player is now playing the real game.

### 4.2 What is *not* taught in the first 60 seconds

Deliberately deferred — introduce each on its own ~2–5 minute arc later:

- Refurbishment (Clean/Repair/Restore) — introduce Day 2 when a damaged item appears in the back inventory.
- Reputation tiers — introduce at first threshold crossing with a PA chime.
- Other four stores — unlock one at a time across Days 2–5.
- Warranty, grading, pack-opening, late fees — each store's signature mechanic gets its own scripted first-encounter.
- Events, meta shifts, seasonal trends — week 2+.
- Difficulty modifiers — exposed via a New Game screen, not mid-play.

### 4.3 Objective rail spec

- Location: top-left, under a "Today" header.
- Max 3 visible items; older completed items fade out after 2 seconds.
- Each item: checkbox glyph, imperative verb + concrete noun, ≤ 6 words.
- Completed items get a one-frame green flash + a soft chime.
- Rail is always dismissable via a collapse arrow, but is never auto-dismissed in the first 10 minutes.
- Rail copy is content (JSON), not embedded in .gd — consistent with the architecture rules.

Suggested JSON shape (`game/content/onboarding/day_one_rail.json`):

```json
{
  "id": "day_one_rail",
  "version": 1,
  "steps": [
    { "id": "open_store",  "text": "Open Retro Games",        "trigger": "storefront_opened",   "arg": "retro_games" },
    { "id": "stock_item",  "text": "Stock 1 item",             "trigger": "item_stocked",        "arg": "retro_games" },
    { "id": "set_price",   "text": "Set a price and open up",  "trigger": "store_doors_opened",  "arg": "retro_games" },
    { "id": "first_sale",  "text": "Make your first sale",     "trigger": "item_sold",           "arg": "retro_games" },
    { "id": "end_day",     "text": "End Day 1 with $40+",      "trigger": "day_closed",          "arg": { "min_cash": 40 } }
  ]
}
```

Wire via `EventBus` signals (`item_sold`, `day_closed`, etc.) — no direct node refs, consistent with architecture rule 2.

### 4.4 Contextual prompt spec

- Rendered as a speech bubble anchored to a world node or UI anchor.
- One sentence, ≤ 90 characters, verb-first.
- Auto-dismiss on the action it describes; timeout fallback at 12 seconds with a gentle re-pulse.
- Voiced by the store's owner NPC where possible — leverages the store-signature identity the game already has.
- Never blocks input. No "Press OK."

### 4.5 The "what changed?" checklist for every onboarding action

Every scripted action in the first 60 seconds must emit all three:

| Action | Visual | Audio | Text |
|---|---|---|---|
| Storefront opens | Grate lifts, lights brighten | Grate rattle + lights hum | — |
| Item stocked | Sprite slots into shelf with bounce | Soft "chunk" | "+1 stocked" float |
| Price set | Tag appears on item with price | Marker squeak | — |
| Doors open | "OPEN" sign flip animation | Door chime | PA: "Now open!" |
| First sale | Coin particles to wallet, wallet counter rolls | Register ka-ching | "+$24" float, PriceResolver ghost line |
| Day closed | Curtain fade, summary panel | Soft closing-bell tone | Summary numbers |

### 4.6 Accessibility and skip behavior

- **Skip.** Holding Esc for 1 second during the intro skips to the "End Day 1 with $40+" state with inventory and shelf pre-populated. Don't offer a one-click skip on first play — offer it on *second* play (detect via save file presence).
- **Subtitles.** PA and NPC speech bubbles are always text-first; audio is supplementary.
- **Color independence.** Rail checkmarks and highlight rings use shape+motion, not color alone.
- **Pacing.** No hard time limits in the first 60 seconds. Timers for scripted NPC arrival pause if the player opens a menu.

### 4.7 Telemetry hooks (later, not v1)

When analytics are added, instrument:
- Time-to-first-stock
- Time-to-first-sale
- Rail step dwell time (per step)
- Rate of Day 1 completion
- Rate of Day 2 start

These are the canonical retention predictors for this genre; a 60-second intro that doesn't move these numbers is worth iterating on.

---

## 5. Condensed checklist

- [ ] One lit affordance at a time, on a world object, not a UI button.
- [ ] Persistent 1–3 item rail, verb-first, auto-advancing, on real numbers.
- [ ] Contextual speech bubbles anchored to objects; no modal walls.
- [ ] Scripted first sale within 60 seconds, oversized feedback, no haggle loss.
- [ ] Triad feedback (visual + audio + text) on every scripted action.
- [ ] Hide locked systems; don't gray them.
- [ ] Hand off to a scoped-but-open goal at second 60 ("End Day 1 with $40+").
- [ ] Defer every non-core system — each gets its own scripted first-encounter later.
- [ ] Rail copy lives in JSON, wired via `EventBus`.
- [ ] Skip path exists but is gated to second-play.

---

## 6. References to existing Mallcore research to cross-load

Before implementing, load these sibling research docs from `docs/research/` (do not re-research):

- `management-hub-ui-patterns.md` — drawer/hub specifics that constrain where the rail and bubbles can live.
- Any `price_resolver_*` or `audit_*` research — determines the format of the ghost audit line in the first-sale beat.
- Any store-signature research for Retro Games — confirms that condition is the right single axis to expose on Day 1.

The recommendation is: **Retro Games is the correct Day 1 store** because its signature mechanic (refurbishment) is deferrable, leaving a clean stock→price→sell loop to teach first. The other four stores each introduce an additional axis (packs, rentals/late-fees, warranty, grading) that would overload the first 60 seconds.
